one_line_takeaway: PerTA replaces a single global damping scalar in task-arithmetic LLM unlearning with per-parameter weights derived from one-time forget/retain gradient (or squared-gradient/Fisher) magnitudes, substantially closing the forgetting-vs-retention gap of vanilla task arithmetic on TOFU/MUSE benchmarks while keeping task arithmetic's efficiency edge over iterative training-based unlearning methods -- this is a model-editing/ML-methods contribution with a privacy motivation, not an adversarial security paper.
key_finding: Table 1 (averaged over the 1%/5%/10% unlearning tasks): on Llama-3.2 1B, vanilla TV reaches FQ=-6.174, MU=0.495, ES(Df)=0.059, ES(Dr)=0.207 versus ground truth FQ=0.000, MU=0.596, ES(Df)=0.064, ES(Dr)=0.748. PerTA-grad improves this to FQ=-0.686, MU=0.556, ES(Dr)=0.376; PerTA-fisher reaches FQ=-0.867, MU=0.562, ES(Dr)=0.414 -- both far closer to ground truth than TV while keeping ES(Df) in a similar low range (0.072-0.080 vs TV's 0.059). On the 3B model, TV gets FQ=-5.284, MU=0.612, ES(Dr)=0.304; PerTA-grad reaches FQ=-0.669, MU=0.664, ES(Dr)=0.563, and PerTA-fisher reaches FQ=-1.211, MU=0.665, ES(Dr)=0.613. The paper frames this as PerTA 'nearly doubling' TV's retain-set performance on the harder 5%/10% tasks (Fig. 5 discussion) while narrowing the MU gap to ground truth to within 0.04 on the 1B model. Training-based baselines degrade sharply on harder tasks: at the 1%-task level GA/NPO/NPO+ are competitive with GT on MU, but averaged across all three tasks GA's MU collapses to 0.199 (1B) / 0.383 (3B) with FQ around -81. Runtime analysis (Fig. 7, not tabulated numerically in the extracted text) shows PerTA retains task arithmetic's efficiency advantage over the best training-based baseline NPO+, with the gap widening as the forget-set proportion grows; using only 20% of the forget/retain data to estimate W already gives results close to using the full data (Fig. 9/12).
core_contribution: The paper proposes Per-parameter Task Arithmetic (PerTA), a training-free LLM unlearning method that rescales the standard task-vector (TV) subtraction per-parameter instead of by a single uniform scalar. Vanilla task arithmetic unlearns by computing theta_final = theta_full + [-(theta_fgt - theta_0)], i.e. subtracting the parameter delta of a model finetuned only on the forget set from the full model. The authors show empirically (Fig. 2a) that for most of the top-30 largest-magnitude entries of this negated task vector, the direction aligns with the gradient-ascent direction of the retain set, causing over-forgetting, and (Fig. 2b) that a single uniform damping weight omega cannot fix this because the TV/retain-gradient relationship varies per parameter. PerTA instead computes theta_final = theta_full + W ⊙ [-(theta_fgt - theta_0)], where W is a per-parameter weight matrix built from one-time gradient computations on the forget set (g_f) and retain set (g_r) at the origin model theta_0: W_grad = (|g_f|+eps)/(|g_r|+|g_f|+2eps), or W_fisher using squared gradients as a diagonal-Fisher-information proxy. The paper proves (Proposition 1) that W_fisher pushes weights closer to 0/1 than W_grad (a cleaner edit/preserve separation), and generalizes both to a family f_oprt(g_f,g_r) = |g_f|^tau / (|g_f|^tau + |g_r|^tau), with tau=1 and tau=2 recovering PerTA-grad and PerTA-fisher.
threat_model: None. This is a model-editing/machine-learning methods paper, not an adversarial security paper. There is no attacker, no adversarial capability assumption, and no defense-vs-attack evaluation. The paper's motivation (LLMs 'may inadvertently disclose sensitive information') is a privacy rationale for why unlearning is useful, but the paper itself studies only benign, non-adversarial forgetting/retention trade-offs on synthetic benchmarks (TOFU is built from fictional GPT-4-generated author biographies; MUSE-News is real BBC news). No membership-inference, extraction, jailbreak, or relearning attack is mounted against PerTA's unlearned models within the main text I reviewed (an appendix section C.6 is titled 'Results under quantization attacks,' referencing prior work on unlearning fragility under quantization, but this was not covered by the pages extracted and is not part of the core method or main results).
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) 'Pruning' (hard top-k masking of TV) performs poorly on harder tasks (10% unlearning); 'Random' weighting has very high variance; a single constant-weighted TV can work but is 'highly sensitive to the hyperparameter' choice of omega. (2) On the simplest task (1% unlearning, small 1B model), 'the advantage of PerTA is not yet pronounced' (Appendix C.2). (3) PerTA-grad can degenerate toward a uniform weight of 0.5 'in some undesirable cases where the gradients on the forget set and the retain set are very similar' (Appendix B.2), reducing its discriminative power relative to PerTA-fisher. (4) The method still requires first training a FgtOnly model (theta_fgt) via standard finetuning (20 epochs on TOFU) before task arithmetic is applied, so it is not training-free end-to-end, only avoiding the iterative retain-aware training loop of methods like NPO+. (5) All core results are on TOFU, a synthetic fictional-author benchmark, and MUSE-News; no discussion in the extracted pages of unlearning general/real-world PII rather than benchmark-constructed entities, and no adversarial robustness evaluation (e.g., relearning or extraction attacks against the unlearned model) appears in the main text.

datasets_used: TOFU (locuslab/TOFU): 4000 QA pairs from 200 fictional, GPT-4-generated author autobiographies; unlearning tasks at 1%, 5%, 10% forget-set proportions
MUSE-News (muse-bench/MUSE-News): BBC news articles published after August 2023
quantitative_results: Table 1 (avg): GA MU collapses to 0.199 (1B) / 0.383 (3B) with FQ approx -81, i.e. severe over-forgetting under training-based GA across the averaged tasks
Table 2 (1% unlearning, 1B): TV FQ=-0.393, MU=0.556, ES(Dr)=0.358; PerTA-grad FQ=-0.289, MU=0.581, ES(Dr)=0.551; PerTA-fisher FQ=-0.576, MU=0.586, ES(Dr)=0.600; GT FQ=0.000, MU=0.599, ES(Dr)=0.751

Proposition 1 (proved in Appendix B.2): for retain-forget ratio r_i = (|g_r|_i+eps)/(|g_f|_i+eps), when r_i>=1, 1/2 >= w_grad_i >= w_fisher_i >= 0; when r_i<1, 1/2 < w_grad_i < w_fisher_i < 1 -- i.e. Fisher weighting always separates edit/preserve parameters more sharply than gradient weighting
baselines_compared: Training-based: GA (gradient ascent on forget set), GD (GA + retain-set gradient descent), NPO (negative preference optimization), NPO+ (NPO + GD)
Task-arithmetic: vanilla TV (Ilharco et al. 2023 task vectors applied to unlearning)
References only (not baselines): Full model (pre-unlearning) and GT (ground-truth model trained only on the retain set)

Ablations against alternative f_oprt designs: Pruning (hard top-lambda% masking), Random (uniform random weights), Weighted (constant omega), SoftMax-based weighting, and a tau-sweep (tau in {0,0.25,0.5,1,2,4,8}) of the general power-ratio form
